commentBody:
Did I make it?

I did buzz through this. Felt like I was on the same wavelength.

"You shared my top-secret chili recipe!"
"Sorry to spill the beans."

("Now I'm stewing about it.")
Golly. I didn’t even start till a few minutes after the hour. I guess I needed a fairly easy one tonight. I’m glad it wasn’t tough. Thanks.

I refuse to believe I’m this good. 
The mob is going to say “too easy” 
After yesterday’s “too hard”. 
Can’t please anyone. 

The long entries just flowed like water. 
I SRSLY hate the abbreviation SRSLY. I hate it. 

Okay. I have a 2005 viral video I need to watch. 

Peace.
I enjoyed the long entries that defined this puzzle. ICANONLYIMAGINE the LINEOFREASONING that led to the clue for FANINTERFERENCE.
NO(ise) COMPLAINTS for me on this one. It wasn’t easy for me, but it felt very fair with few naticks and some nice long fill. More please :)

Wow did my comment really get emu’d? Try again:
I thought FAN INTERFERENCE was just trying to grab a home run ball in play. I think a streαker would be Fαnnγ Interference.

I don’t feel like repeating all my other musings again, maybe they’ll turn up. Suffice it to say, breezy but entertaining with several clever misdirects, some more subtle than others.

I'm not familiar with the meme, but if you give me four of the five words from a LOTR quote, I'm probably getting the fifth word without context.

I know that unagi means EELS from Friends, but wouldn't the main ingredient if unagi don (or any don) be rice?

SPILLEDTHEBEANS was an easy spanner and gave me a nice toehold
Banger
Ah. Flow state for me. Perfect amount of frustration and aha moments. A+

Wow! This was just great. Kept me guessing. Just when I thought I was stumped in one area, I'd see a wrong answer and get started again. 

Great construction and great clueing. Dollar offering had me until the very end.

This was a tough puzzle but a rewarding one.  I got the top half quick but every part of the bottom half was a bit of a struggle.

THEYRE was sneaky, I was looking for words that came from the trilogy.  (Since it was a 'Viral video' I didn't think it had to be an actual direct quote).

A few I didn't know but had enough crossings to reason it out like ANTEATER as relative of sloth.

Really well clued spanners like LINE OF REASONING, ESPRESSO MACHINE, FAN INTERFERENCE.

COIFS/SRSLY gave me a lot of trouble, that was what I got last.  I know what 'No cap' means but thought the clue was translating it to a normal word, not an even youthier youth slang.

First, and less importantly, this seemed preposterously easy and pedestrian for a Friday, but maybe that's just me.  (Looks as if XWStats shows Average at present.)

Much more alarming, the Archive page is NOT working properly for me today.  It refused to show the Friday icon after a refresh.  It actually blinked on and then off again.  I had to navigate in another way.  After completing the puzzle it's showing up in Archive, so at least there's that.  Again, maybe just me.  This is just earth shaking.  ;)

Hope all are well.

What a good, good Friday puzzle, the longs smoothly clued and plenty of gnarly areas to give one pause, like the clue for HUNT or what accompanies waffles, I dig Willa's twisty mind, her line of unreasoning. 

It's funny how some clues are Rorschachs that reveal something of our selves. When I was first pondering what late night partying might lead to, the first things I thought of were hangovers, headaches, missed appointments, and in general the ill tidings of a profligate lifestyle. I didn't think of romantic hookups, boogie nights, having fun with friends on the dance floor, doing a dozen jello shots, or getting a tattoo of a favorite Pokemon on my posterior the way some of you probably did. 

Or like yesterday, I couldn't for the life of me think of a hit 1977 musical with FDR in it. I mused to my wife, "Was there a hit musical in the late seventies called YALTA, with FDR, Churchill and Stalin?" There's no such musical, she replied, maybe you should consider pitching it to your silly theater friends, they're always looking for ways to waste money. By the way, it's ANNIE.

SCAD seems a little iffy to me, but I haven't looked it up on MW or OED to see if it's a failing on my part, that I've only seen its plural. Something like SCAD came up recently, or maybe it WAS scad. I don't have scads of brain cells left any more. And I do like SOFT for [Merely whispered] though it took a while for my brain to fully accept it, and even now I can hear some mutterings.

"Things that come with waffles?" 

Can some explain "ers" to me?

All that's running through my mind is that waffles are a massive choking hazard and everyone ends up in the "ER". 

After decades of eating waffles safely, I now feel incredibly lucky. 🤷‍♀️

Grandma, Grandpa, what's a typewriter? SRSLY, the typewriters settings clue for DESKS was the one that flummoxed me the longest, otherwise, No Notes. For the LOTR fans out there, here's Steven Colbert's homage to the 20th anniversary of the release of the first movie from a bunch of years ago, which I've posted before. It's way shorter tgan 10 hours. Warning there are a few instances of NSFW words scattered throughout. https://youtu.be/L1dU1HZ_73M?si=AJw0JC_yLAjsD0l5

This was hard! at first, and I had to go all over the place to add a speculative letter here and there — or remove one, as the case may was. (C'mon, that *ought was grammatical.) But little by little — littlerally — the gestalt gestalted and words slowly began to reveal themselves. And my solve time wasn't that much longer than my Friday average. There were many great clues, Saturday-worthy, perhaps. Like "Camp neckwear" for BOAS, where camp don't mean summer camp, but campy. Or "Yard sticks?" for FENCE. And the six 15s always make a puzzle fun. It seemed ironic that "In tandem" clued AS ONE, while it might also clue "AS TWO". And I was awestruck when at a conference in 1983 I found myself in Detroit and visited the Detroit Institute of Arts and saw the Rivera murals. Especially considering that his Man at the Crossroads mural (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Man_at_the_Crossroads#/media/File:Mural._El_hombre_en_el_cruce_de_caminos._Diego_rivera_(1934).JPG) was destroyed almost as soon as it was completed, these murals are well worth a visit. (There are also some great murals in the manner of Rivera, in Coit Tower in San Francisco.)
literally took me an hour and 45 mins
Liked it!

Super quiet in the comments tonight. 
Versus the fireworks yesterday. 

I was pretty active in the comments last night and this morning. But I did notice the top reader pick for most of the day was a long post from Francis
Grand Marais, MN. And now it’s gone.  Like it got removed by mods?   Now I’m curious. I guess a sub-thread devolved into madness.  But I feel like the mods can just delete sub-threads now, versus the entire post.  

What did I miss? Come on. Tell me…

Tonight’s comment section is boring.  No fighting.  No grand statements.  No arguments over definitions or arcana.  
Just a bunch of reasonable, non controversial posts.  Boring…

I’m here for the drama. Haha
And I’m back to a 1 day streak! Thanks again Thursday.

Nice puzzle. Somehow BEAVERS were a gimme for me as lodge builders - I did not realize I actually knew that meaning of the word "lodge". The Polish word for a beaver lodge is the very specific term "żeremie", which applies to nothing else. It derives from the noun "żer", "animal food". Incidentally, that noun in turn gave rise to two verbs: "żerować" and "żreć". The former means "to eat", but only as an animal. The latter is an uncouth descriptor for the action of a person (or more rarely, an animal) eating greedily and noisily. Beavers had become nearly extinct in Poland, but conservation efforts in recent decades have made a huge difference - beavers have become practicality ubiquitous in wet areas. Several months ago they actually built a dam on a very urban stream dividing my district of Ursynów from Mokotów to the North, transforming the landscape. The spot has become a huge attraction, with people coming from all over Warsaw to see it. Naturalists have been praising the environmental impact of the dam - the resulting reservoir has improved local water conditions and has become a habitat for many plants and animals, not just beavers. Here is what it looked like in April: https://imgur.com/a/DKdSyHB Before in that spot there was only a rivulet a meter wide.
This was a good one! Happy Friday, y’all!
Great fill and great cluing. What a pleasure.
So much better than yesterday's. What makes a crossword hard/fun is the wordplay, not trivia.
Similar grid design to one of my favourite themelesses from last year (7 June 2025) by Mr Slonecker, loads of fun

A complaint about the wordplay column: i use an iPhone and I can normally see Wordplay in my favourites, along with the most recent stories I’ve looked at. 

On Thursday Wordplay was hidden chronologically behind Wednesday’s column, and today i couldn’t see it at all. I had to click on Sean’s byline and then click on the link to Wordplay in his bio. 

Now that I’ve looked at the Friday Wordplay once it should show up in my most recents, but it’s not there. No wonder not many people are commenting. They probably can’t find it!

The only thing better than "THEY'RE Taking the Hobbits to Isengard" showing up is the fact that I just happened to spend a couple of hours earlier tonight reading all about how sloths are related to ANTEATERS and armadillos. I'm so pleased. Sloths are crazy! Look up the extinct giant ground sloth sometime and all the other strange creatures (like marsupials) that originated in South America and traveled across the Antarctic land bridge.
I dislike fish (as food) and EEL sounds especially disgusting to me, so with that and AHI and SEABASS all in the same puzzle, I was making some faces, but otherwise it was a pretty good puzzle.
A pleasant 15-minute themeless Wednesday.
Excellent example of the puzzling art of puzzling. Bravo.
After SEABASS, EEL and AHI I will trade that WETBAR for a rawbar....
Fun and fast

Many lovely moments in the outing today.  Such as:
•	Reading a clue, having a short or long moment of confusion, then the sudden sweet joy/relief of cracking it.  This happened with the “waffles”, “hot shot”, and “game” misdirects.
•	My two favorite answers, and they cross – WHAT IFS and I CAN ONLY IMAGINE.
•	A smile at the apt abutting of EEK and BOAS.  I think I heard the EEK.

Five box-spanners combined with a low number of black squares (27), and yet a practically junk-free answer set, revealing a high level of constructing skill.  That, combined with the lovely wordplay scattered throughout, made this puzzle a showcase of the art and science of creating crosswords.

Sufficient rub, plenty of fun – a most splendid outing.  Thank you, Willa!
It took me a while, but it was worth it. I enjoyed  this much more than yesterday's "old-fashioned Thursday," or whatever a few people were calling it. But no puzzle could ever please everyone.

Fun one!
Went by quickly,
but forced me to abandon
my solving pattern,

which is *always* a good sign.

True story- 
we ate Swedish waffles (very thin) last night 
along with Swedish pancakes (verrry thin),

after painting our fence!

And our anteaters are snuggled up
right next to me as I solve.

Not lying,
I just pronounce “anteater”
as “PUP.”

All the letters are silent.
I just spent an inordinate amount of time trying to make “Branzino” an accomplished player of “SKA BASS”. 🤦‍♂️
Did anyone else have "Let's Go" as the clue for 45A?  And if so, how is HAHAS the answer?

Quick, but surprisingly fun.

I think Sean has it right:  "Fresh, stylish fill, escorted by snappy clues that feature late night partying, hot shot producers and a streaker running across the lower third of the grid."

I'd have preferred it if it offered greater resistance, but I'll take it.
Thanks, Willa Angel Chen Miller!
I loved it! The right level of challenging and fun. Espresso machine was brilliant.
I've never given up on a puzzle so quickly. Just asinine cluing and crazy long answers.  I got maybe 9 answers right. And the rest were just a crazy slog. Not enjoyable in the slightest

Quite hard. A few cultural clashes. Some of the words I didn't understand have already been mentioned, like the RENTAL CAR clue. But 'yuks' gives HAHAS? Not heard of that, only yuck, expressing disgust, nor coneheads. 

Crosswords can make me feel so ignorant. A viral video from 2005? I've never seen any viral video, anytime!

Not familiar with a WET BAR, though I think it's come up before, and I looked it up. I've never, ever been in a house with a bar, let alone a wet bar with its own sink. It's not the case now, but it used to be that if you drank at home, it was assumed you had no friends to go to the pub with! This was before people drank wine. 

And SALAD as 'intro course'? For me, having a salad as a first course would be strange. 

Some very nice clues that I could solve though - I liked SMEARED, FENCE, ENABLED.

15-minute Fridays fray my forbearance for foolishness.  And I care nothing for speed.  I want a struggly, sweaty, trekky late-week puzzle.  Not a Wednesday.

I'm developing a sense of pseudo-second sight whereby I imagine I'm seeing where and how the editors dumb down clues even as I solve.  Maybe that's a sign I should try constructing?  But someone here would just dumb that down, too, were it good enough to make public.  Unless maybe it's a Monday?

And though I'm sure I'm getting better blah blah, I'm not getting leaps-and-bounds better; at this point the improvement is simply asymptotic.

The archives prove this beyond a doubt.

Happy to get 'er done, even though it took 29% longer than average.  The bottom two spanners and some of their crosses gave me fits.  Surely FANINTERFERENCE is a debut; if it's a "thing" I've never heard of it.  ENABLED for "on", though accurate, is still pretty diabolical (YMMV).  Though I suspected HAHAS, it still took a while to come up with HUNT.  Again, just happy to get the music.

Well.. typical tough Friday for me and had to look up some stuff. Not much else to say. And... my usual puzzle find: A Sunday from April 30, 1995 by Warren W. Reich with the title: "Animal Fare." Some theme answers: OTTERNONSENSE GNUSPAPERS LIONDRIVE JACKALLANTERN DEERSANTA BURROOFQUEENS BEARBONES RABBITTRANSIT Here's that link: https://www.xwordinfo.com/Crossword?date=4/30/1995&h=34d ....
I can only imagine how difficult this was to make. And such a beautiful looking grid with 6 spanners to work out. Perfect Friday for me.
Loved SPILLEDTHEBEANS crossing ESPRESSOMACHINE
Compared with yesterday's puzzle, this was a very satisfying challenge, although I suspect some found it too easy for a Friday.
Great grid-spanning clues and answers that unfortunately come at the cost of some poor fill. Happy Friday!

Lively grid-spanners, nicely clued.  My favorite was LINE OF REASONING which I got with 2 or 3 crosses.  And I'm so glad I didn't write in NOISE violatIons when I thought of it; it would have loused up the whole top of the puzzle.

A new way to clue OLE, but not one I especially like.

I finally got NRC, but I'm old enough to remember the AEC.

GRE before GED.

I didn't know who was taking the Hobbits somewhere or other and I didn't care.  I expected a name, but happily it wasn't a name.

Is SCAD really "ginormous" enough?

HAHA, I don't know what catfishing is, but you'll never get me with that or any other SCAM.  I don't open up strange emails and I don't stay on the phone long enough (less than 3 seconds) to find out what it is you want to alarm me about.

A colorful puzzle that gave me some trouble from time to time.  I enjoyed it.

I appreciate this puzzle enormously. It took a bit to find a toehold, and I solved it fairly chaotically, bouncing around the grid. I was tempted a couple times to look something up, but I managed to work through the ones I had no idea about, and finished around 25% under my average time. Thanks to the constructor for a pleasant start to my day.
About an hour for me today. Probably the low end of my normal Friday range. A really fun fill!

I started out like a bat on out of hell in the top third, and thought, okay, here's another Monday puzzle. Then got my comeuppance when I got to the middle, and found the bottom third the hardest of all. But then it was done.

I did appreciate the relative absence of obscure trivia, unlike yesterday, and some really clever clueing (cluing?), such as [Hot shot producer] and [Stylish, as an award show]. But even though I I've seen it before and didn't remember it until I got most of the crosses, I don't understand how the abbreviation SRSLY relates to No Cap. I'd a thunk it that it came from the Italian Non capisco, but it doesn't.

If you've been on as many cruises as I have, branzino is a common entree (main course for you non-Americans). Most of the time what they call Chilean sea bass here is fake, but I'm not sure about the fish served on ships. All I know is that it's delicious, whatever it is.

Started around 7:30 and finished just after 9. Lost electricity nine minutes into my solve, so spent more than an hour running errands outside the house. Once power was restored, I finished up in about eight additional minutes. Seemed very easy for a late-week puzzle.

Too many direct clues for a Friday, especially since the six spanners were easy to guess---a 90 box gift just there.

Nice enough construction, but, unlike yesterday's great puzzle, just not very puzzling.
A perfect Friday.  Just hard enough and just long enough.

Had a weird time trying to get here..."Backend Unhealthy"??  I asked DHubby and he checked, said it looked okay to him....

ANYway, this was Quite the... SOME?
I did hear about 'catfishing,' but can't remember exactly how it works...  So many SCAMs, so little time!
Of course, my fave was FAN INTERFERENCE, which I got quite early.  Most helpful!

"Things that come with waffles" = ERS.  Um, uh, hm, [shrug].
I started 14A with IMPRESSARIO and was disappointed by having to disassemble it immediately.  Maybe some other time, eh?

Today's task:  cutting the outer border and measuring and pressing the backing fabric .....then (groan) clearing up the work-station so I can layer the quilt sandwich.  Extra time required in order to ponder how to turn into one of those people who always puts everything away ...

I admired the way so many unapproachable across answers were eased by very approachable downs. 

Until I got to the bottom middle, where I had to think a little. Had to let go of my memory of 'bandeaus' as these plastic hair thingies, which I see you can still buy.

Adored all six spanners.

Full credit for my gold star today goes to my dog.

It's the old story: I was halfway through, frustrated and about to quit, but old Festus needed to go outside, so we went for a short walk in the garden.  We got back in, he settled at my feet under the computer desk and I looked up at the puzzle on my screen and the answer to 3 down appeared out of nowhere.  Then another and another, and in less than 5 minutes I heard the happy music.

It has been said here many times before.  The brain, for some reason, refreshes neural pathways when given a short rest and distraction.  Sunshine and dogs may also help.
A SRSLY excellent Friday puzzle. Well done. ⭐️

If you want to receive all my crossword poems in your inbox you can subscribe at https://substack.com/@petervalentine here’s today’s poem. a/ lo! the mini fridge all night a-running whispered to impulses d/ proceed… proceed to things untapp’d a pathway past the present map to a game plan a/ I can only imagine
Enjoyable with just the right level of hurt for me, like a New Mexico Hatch Chile Relle∆ño, on the bright side. Thanks for the Friday morning wake up call!
46 down has me thinking of Charlotte's Web this morning https://m.youtube.com/watch?v=__7NUrkFirA

Had to check here to understand ERS relationship to waffles.  Guess I, um, need to, ah, open up the old, uh, mind.   With 4 Fridays (2 were collabs) our constructor is in the groove.   Care to comment?  Well done.

Well Thursday was Saturday level and Friday is Wednesday level but I liked this puzzle. Not much clever cluing but  I'll take a straight forward puzzle after yesterday. And "hot shot producer" was pretty sharp.
Our cockapoo Tillie dragged me out for a walk when I was totally stalled halfway through. 30 minutes out with her in the very warm Virginia morning broke the ice jam and we drifted into the music.

Uuuuugggghhhh..

Seriously?!?!? What else could the answer be???
So annoying.

Light and breezy. Thanks, Willa.
That felt like the toughest puzzle I ever finished quickly.
Branzino held me up for quite a while. What the actual? Did Brangelina break up? Oh, it's a fish. Yeah, I don't even look at that section of the menu. And I thought they were calling it tilapia.
what a xw should be. excellent Friday puzzle. bravo!

As it so happens, to pUNT or to bUNT could also be ways to proceed with a game plan 😭 pahahaha

This was a fun one. Excellent Friday puzzle! And yes, now the Hobbit song will be in my head all day.

For me, a nice chewy Friday in exactly average Friday time.

I asked my wife for help with branzino (I was thinking cod for some reason) and she looked it up...in the latest Zingerman's catalog! lol

One quibble: Wu Zetian wasn't an empress, she was a straight up emperor, with the same Chinese title as all the other (male) emperors who reigned before and after her. The only woman to ever hold that title in Chinese history!

This one felt kind of hard to me but it turns out I finished about 32 point something percent faster than my average. Maybe it was the extra birthday martini I allowed myself last night...  Well, what else are you going to do when you're eating at your favorite Russian restaurant!? Don't worry, there were no NOISECOMPLAINTS.  Had a beautiful evening on the restaurant patio with wonderful food and even better company! 🥳  

Anyhow, I thought it was a good puzzle with some fun word play. HUNT was clued so cutely that I couldn't even turn my hunting hater nose up at it. 😏  At first I actually thought, that can't be right, when I filled it in with the crosses...  but then the nickel dropped and I said my HAHAS. 

I also really liked the spanners!  Good puzzle! Felt very fresh to me! Isn't it weird some of the words we use for crosswords?  I'm not always sure what makes a puzzle fresh, but I can feel it when it's not. 

Lastly, @NYCTraveler, in case you didn't revisit the comments yesterday, I wanted to thank you for your call out and birthday greetings! Very sweet!
"Suffix with caffe-" would be an awful clue even on a Monday. How did that get through?

Seems like at about the same time as the new Games software update, some processes have become much slower.  It is taking approximately 26 seconds from clicking the link in the comment notification email until the time the comment is displayed in the comments section.
Great puzzle today.

Most of mine went smoothly but then did a lot of word-checks in the Mid-West.
Thanks for a pleasant one today!
Bottom third was nice and difficult. Good puzzle!
Boy this was frustrating, and not in the fun way.
THIS is the kind of puzzle I come here on Saturdays for. Great job Willa Angel Chen Miller!

commentBody: wu zetian is of the tang dynasty, not zhou. Unless i misunderstood?

“…come with waffles”???
????,

Is it just me or is "Yuks" a contronym? For me, it means
A) the sounds made on observing something gross and repellent or 
B) the sounds of laughter or the jokes that engender them.
This clue obviously used B but I think A is legit.

I think a lightly grilled branzino pairs well with a dry bandeau.

Two words I had only heard before today.

Nice puzzle today. 7 minutes better than average so, yay me. I do have an observation though. Yesterday at this time there were probably close to 600 or more comments. The boo birds sure come out when a puzzle is above their skill set. Yet a nice medium to slightly difficult one like today’s gets no kudos. So,  kudos from me. Well done Willa!

Speaking of 36A [Creator of the Detroit Industry Murals] Diego Rivera, I haven't seen those murals but I have seen several films about Diego Rivera and his wife, painter Frida Kahlo, one starring Selma Hayak.

Poor Miss Kahlo suffered a terrible terrible accident when she was young. The bus she was riding on crashed and....well, I don't want to upset you but it was bad.

She had terrible pain all her life and her art work amazingly reflects that part of her. 

Her mother devised a way that she could paint while lying down, by suspending an easel above her when she was on her back in the bed. Sometimes I wish I had one of those too, to facilitate my paint-by-number hobby.

Wow! I started this puzzle earlier this morning and barely made any progress. I figured it would be the typical Friday. I came to the column and read the comments about it being breezy and thought what the hell!? So I took a long break and when I came back 14A and 17A came to me. Then (maybe cause I was on the right wavelength 11D came to me and then I LABORED no more… 

Great Friday puzzle!

Rating:
9/10
"They're Taking the Hobbits to  Isengard" was something viral? I missed it completely. And the link was to a 10 hour version?! I am once again confirmed that I'm old. Great crossword, by the way.
I so loved the clues today!! I found them very tricky and fresh. Totally needed to put it down for awhile to let my brain bend into the solves. I leaned some stuff too.

It's a simple hack but watching what I type more closely has been a game changer for me. I used to make so many typos and it was killing my time. Going more slowly actually helps me finish the puzzle more quickly.

I'm sure many of you already figured this out but I thought I'd share.

Also, great puzzle today.
What a great puzzle !
Today's puzzle was hilarious. A nice way to end the work week. Thank you! (And now our household is singing "They're Taking the Hobbits to Isengard")
Lots of HAHAS with this one, I had a great time
Fun themeless and way easier than Thursday’s stumper

A follow up to my one-line, smiley emoticon, below. The Detroit Industries murals were commissioned by the then director of the Detroit Institute of Arts, William Valentiner, the commission paid for by Henry Ford's son Edsel Ford*. Supposedly, Rivera and Ford had a good working relationship, despite one being a staunch socialist, and the other about as capitalist as one can be. Portraits of Valentiner and Ford are worked into the corner of one of the panels, in the manner of Venetian nobles and clergy in a Renaissance altarpiece. While he was working on the murals, Rivera's wife Frieda Kahlo stayed with him in Detroit. Rivera loved the city; Kahlo, not so much. Still, Detroit likes to make a big fuss about her time here. Here's a webpage with better images than the one in Wordplay: https://historicdetroit.org/galleries/detroit-institute-of-arts-photos-industry-murals *Now, wouldn't that be a better way of cluing EDSEL than [Notable 1950's flop]?

I have been solving but not commenting much - this puzzle was so good, I had to get on here.  Very satisfying and clever, I especially love getting the spanner ones.  I especially like the puzzles where you get vast majority of the entries just from the clueing.  Too good. SRSLY.
SRSLY great clues and yet painfully awkward clues too. Blech
Took me longer than usual, but very satisfying to power through without hints.
I didn't think I'd finish without banging my head against a wall and I proved myself wrong!!
What does SCAD mean?

"Not be square with" doesn't quite seem to work grammatically as a clue for OWE.  I can't see a way to use it as a direct substitution. "Am not square with" would be better. There were (imo) a few such clues that didn't quite seem to work.

Weak,
I saw your question yesterday hours after you posted it. 
My solve: when the puzzle drops, I print the Newspaper Version PDF and do it with a pen. End of story. I don't key it in later. If I think -- or read in the column or comments -- that there is an animation. I'll either fill the puzzle on the website to see it or just go to xwordinfo.com. Only exception is the Sunday crossword, which I do in the Magazine after I receive it Saturday morning. I have the stone tablets delivered to my cave seven days a week; I support what's left of the press.
@Barry Ancona, most admirable that you let Weak slide on their remarks about SRSLY, considering your fondness for it. Restraint, thy name is Ancona.

@Weak

He said that he solves with pen.
Sorry, Weak. With it in the puzzle at 58A, I had to use it.
@B I chortled

@Andrzej

I do the same, having been burned too many times in the past.

@SP,
Granted, they don’t play baseball in the UK (typically), but under no circumstances would they ever refer to such streakers as being Fanny interference!

@Chris
I was looking for an LOTR character whose name fit the crossings I had. Needless to say, there is none. (I was not assisted by confidently filling in phd and cropTOP as crossings.)

@B

I finished this in Wednesday time, in the end, but I still think it was (mostly) a worthy Friday grid - it took me a long time to get footholds that allowed me to ultimately deal with the fill. After my first pass, I had almost nothing. On some level this is what I enjoy most - a puzzle hard enough to initially make me think I will get nowhere with it, but clued so well it actually allows things to fall into place quite naturally. And of course I enjoyed how little trivia there was. Also, I think this was the first puzzle in several days that featured nothing from the "sport" of golf - that's reason enough to celebrate, for me.

@Andrzej nice photo!

@Kris H

Note the apartmentment buildings rising above the trees in the background. Similar ones are out of shot to the right. This is basically the middle of a huge city. When the residential districts were being built in the 1970s and 1980s, city planners preserved some of the natural landscape, like the valley of this stream here, in stead of building over everything. There are also many green spaces between the buildings themselves - strangely enough, the ruling autocrats did care about providing people with high living standards, especially for the day.

@Andrzej 
Amazing how Nature works in harmony when allowed to. And we humans, with only a few thousand years of building and engineering experience, seem to think we know better.

@Andrzej

If someone else had posted this you'd be responding with all sorts of innuendos about beavers and blockages and wetlands, wouldn't you?

@Andrzej

Fascinating! If that happened in my city, I guarantee an outcry about unsightly debris gumming up the stream would have that dam removed, pronto. It’s nice to know there are still cities that let nature be nature.

@Matt

If you or Francis had posted it, sure, duh.

@Andrzej 

My house in central Atlanta has a small rivulet in back of it that runs at the bottom of a forty-foot-deep gully and feeds into Entrenchment Creek, so named because of its role during our Civil War. Before the neighborhood was established in the 19th century it was farmland, but I believe the steep gully banks left that area impossible to farm. There are oak trees there that are at least eighty feet tall and may be a small surviving pocket of primeval forest. I have owls, woodpeckers, snakes, squirrels, salamanders, raccoons, rabbits, and a chorus of migrating  songbirds that changes daily, all within sight of my back wall.

And somehow, miles inside of an urban srea of close to seven million, beavers found their way up Entrenchment Creek a few years ago, so now we have a pond south of us. complete with herons wading slowly through it.

And I live less than ten minutes away from a skyscraper roughly the size of the Chrysler Building in NYC.

@Bruce

That's so great, I'm happy for you! The housing estate my wife and I live at borders an old, English-style park, established on the river Wisła's ancient, Ice-age river bank. The park was largely left alone by people for the whole latter half of the 20th century, so much so that it transformed into a proper forest - these days it is a nature reserve, home to wild boar and deer, and naturalists say it more closely resembles a primeval, Mazovian forest than almost any other woodland area in the region. I pass by Mazowsze's oldest oak tree, called Mieszko, after Poland's earliest attested ruler, a prince of the 10th century, on my daily walks with Lucek the poodle. And all this is 20 minutes by metro from Warsaw's skyscrapers.

@Andrzej
Our NE Ohio home was near a creek that flowed through a culvert under an unpaved road.  Eventually the county paved the road, which brought in more traffic; at about the same time, some beavers industriously dammed the creek, which backed up enough to encroach on the road at its lowest point.  (Insert lengthy back-and-forth of county workers destroying the dam and beavers rebuilding it overnight....)  Eventually the wildlife officer trapped the beaver (but not competently, so the beaver drowned) and that was that...  (The beavers that stuck to the nearby Aurora Branch of the Chagrin River were safer.)

@Super8ing

If you’d like a good example of this I’d recommend checking out Chris Remo on YouTube. He finished today’s in under 12 minutes, and aside from being an adept solver, he’s extremely approachable and explains answers as he’s solving. Watching his videos has changed the way I approach the puzzles.

The Coneheads was a sketch on Saturday Night Live several decades ago in which Jane Curtin and Dan Aykroyd played aliens with bald, cone-shaped heads. They always explained their oddness by saying they were from France. As a Brit, I think you might appreciate that!

As for WET BAR, there are some really big houses over here. 

As for SALAD being an intro course, at weddings and other similar catered events here, appetizers are often served in a different room before moving into the dining hall, during the cocktail hour. They are often passed out by servers to guests who are standing, so this might not be seen as a proper course. At the table, the salad is often the first thing served.

Thanks. 
You’re forgetting that it’s the English who are anti-French, not the Scots. Long ago, Scotland and France were allies (called the Auld Alliance) against England and that still persists. The whole of Scotland was anti-Brexit. 

At weddings it’s nearly always champagne served first. In Scotland it was sherry for the ladies and whisky for the men. I expect it’s now champagne or one of the cheaper fizzy whites, but I can’t imagine whisky will be far away. I’ve never had a salad at a wedding or function, apart from a garnish. 
There isn’t a salad ‘ course’ as such. People might order a salad, as an alternative to the usual course, and are usually seen trying to lose weight.

I’ve seen in some stately homes (where you pay to look round) something called a butler’s kitchen, where drinks are prepared. By the butler. Maybe other big houses have bars too, I’ve just not been in them!
Here, big houses, little houses... https://www.thespruce.com/wet-bar-ideas-5181448

This is one of the transatlantic divides. Eastwards, the salad course, if separate, is served after the fish or the entrée as a palate cleanser. Westwards, the salad is ofren served as a preliminary course.  How and why, I have no idea.

@Charles Peterson

A suffix is a letter or group of letters added to the end of a base word to change its meaning or part of speech. 
Caffe is not a word.
INE for serpent as the previous poster mentioned is a valid suffix, just finishing off the letters of a word to complete its spelling is not.
@Barry Ancona yes those are all legitimate examples of amending an existing word…. Caffe isn’t an English word!
@Barry Ancona not in English it ain’t!
Gareth, https://www.oed.com/dictionary/caffe_n?tl=true

@Gareth

I wasn't going to wade into this but turns out it's in Merriam too.  If we have to accept "kudo" based on citation then I sure as shootin' am going to accept caffe.  (Even as I too thought it was a poor clue.)
@Gareth Yes, it's a suffix. "caffe-" meaning "of coffee" and "-ine" being a common chemical suffix denoting an alkaloid substance. Which caffeine is.

@Gareth, @BA, et @lii--
After yesterday's "kudo,' I just couldn't stay away.
As etymology, www.oed.com lists a borrowing from French "caféine," from "café" + "ine" (a suffix).
www.merriwm-webster.com says it comes from German "Kaffein," from "Kaffe" + "in" (but "Kaffe" isn't a word in German!--it's "Kaffee"!)
www.wiktionary.com says it might come from either of those two, or even Italian "caffè" + "ine".
(But we know that wiktionary is merely a cabal of Italian barristas and Finnish weavers, aimed to deceive us.)
In any case, no good ol' 'Murican suffixes were involved--just those darned foreign ones!

Barry, I'd go with the Mozart, any day.
@Barry Ancona I mean, I'll take the last three, where you aren't literally just given the only possible answer immediately

Patagonian toothfish is branded as Chilean sea bass.  Which is an amazingly rich and buttery fish. And very expensive.  It was crazy popular like 15 years ago. Like overfished.  I distinctly remember being limited by how I could buy from my local fish market, because of demand (and supply). 
It’s still amazing. But I think you can buy as much as you want nowadays, if you can afford the $35-$40 a pound fillet pricing. 

Talapia is a cheaper fish. I call it a bottom feeder, like catfish. 

Branzino is definitely a popular Italian restaurant grilled fish. Yummy.

Lynn,
I have also heard ten weeks for the "recent" averages, but I'm  not a subscriber and I don't know for sure. One note: while they call this one Average, based on their stats I'd call this one Easy (even by current eased standards). 

Difficulty  Average
Median Solve Time  14:45
Median Solver  13% faster
⚡70% of users solved faster than their Friday average.
38% solved much faster (>20%) than their Friday average.
🐢30% of users solved slower than their Friday average.
12% solved much slower (>20%) than their Friday average.

@Lynn

From the site itself:

How are the puzzle difficulties calculated?

A puzzle's difficulty on the puzzle page is determined by looking at the median solver's performance on that puzzle. However much faster or slower that user solved the puzzle compared to their personal 10-week rolling average time on that day is then compared to the median solvers for every puzzle for the past year. The slowest 10% are considered Very Hard, the fastest 10% are considered Very Easy. The next 20% slowest and fastest are considered Hard and Easy, respectively. The middle 40% are considered Average.

This can sometimes be confusing if the puzzle's statistics appear to tell one story but the difficulty says otherwise. For example, a puzzle where the median solver solved 15% faster than their average would actually be considered an Average difficulty puzzle, not an Easy one. This is true because of one simple, yet interesting, fact: solvers normally solve faster than their 10-week rolling average. In other words, more often than not, a solver will solve a puzzle faster than their average time for that day of the week. Therefore, a puzzle where 60% of users solved faster than their average is actually pretty middle of the road when it comes to puzzle difficulty.

Barry,
I was curious about that. The median solve time was a noticeably smaller fraction of my time than usual, and my time was faster than my own average by a reasonable amount.

@Lynn

I had a (TM) and everything.

@Jim

The NYT IT people can do nothing right, can they...

@Andrzej

I wouldn't go that far.  As a former IT professional, I know management (usually non-IT management) causes the most IT problems by having you work on what is often the wrong thing.

@Jim

Sure, but the sheer number of mistakes and their frequency is way beyond anything I have ever experienced in any app, and most of it seems purely technical in nature. It's hard to imagine the IT crew is faultless...

@SBK in TO
Right there with you. Remember Brooks from Shawshank?  The word has gotten itself into a big dam hurry. However, when I click submit and there are no issues, my phone ( I use my iPad to play and write) immediately pings. I close the window and voila there it is. My guess is it’s their phone/tablet or lame internet service.

@SBK in TO

As I mentioned, I was an IT professional, and we dealt with response time complaints all the time.  I noticed it was taking a lot longer than it used to.  I'd rather not spend the rest of my remaining time on earth to wait on something that used to take a fraction of the time.  I am not being unreasonable, but I feel that you are in your jumping to conclusions about how I behave in the rest of my life.

@Jim

I feel you. A family member has incredibly slow wifi (as in it takes a minute or two to load the nyt home page and that's on a good day when it doesn't keep timing out) and whenever I'm there trying to do things online every second waiting for something to (hopefully) load is kind of excruciating. Yes, having to wait a few seconds isn't the end of the world, and I do think it likely that people have gotten more impatient in the modern age, but given how fast things can be (and usually are) done online, having to wait around twiddling my thumbs feels like some sort of psychological experiment or perhaps existential test.
srsly

Down_Home,
I hope you'll enjoy the Saturday puzzle...
@Barry Ancona Oof... I'm retired so lose track of the days 😵‍💫 The only days I'm sure of are Mondays and Thursdays, my shift days at the dog pound 🐶

@wu zetian is of the tang dynasty
I understand that she is of both.  She deposed her son (a Tang) and founded the Zhou and ruled for 15 years (690-705) before abdicating and returning her son to the throne.  The Zhou is considered an interregnum, or temporary, dynasty within the Tang dynasty.
parentUserDisplayName: wu zetian is of the tang dynasty

commentBody:
@G. L. Dryfoos
I couldn’t decide if I wanted syrup, sliced bananas or berries with my waffle so I became a waffler.
@G. L. Dryfoos Think of "waffles" as a verb! If you're waffling, you might be saying uhs, ums or ERS.
Also, I’ve destroyed about four waffle makers over the years  but never thought of them as wafflers. Didn’t call them waffle irons, either.

SBK,
Yuks are funny (yuk yuk); yuck is gross (yucky).
Today is spelling; yesterday was phonetics.
I'd like to be there when you order...

@Bill
One of my favorite elements of the murals is that in the entire series there is only one tiny car, way back in the middle of the last one.
@Jumigely I usually hear it as "scads."  I've never seen or heard it in the singular. It means a lot.
